body_txt: The idea of a monster can mean a lot of things — in fact, we have a whole list of tropes about just that . However, many works of fiction — especially ones aimed at children — seem to default to a very specific kind of generic "monster". When a child is afraid of a beast or a monster or a bogeyman hiding in the attic, stalking them in a dark house, or scowling toothily from behind a corner, the first image in a lot of minds will be quite alike — a sort of apelike or bearlike or trollish thing, hairy and perhaps horned, that stalks the dark hallways of the world's childhood paranoia. These monsters tend to be roughly humanoid in shape, often with long-armed, monstrous proportions. They're usually massive and hairy and heavily built, and may be tricked out with a whole laundry list of additional features like horns, scales, tentacles, fangs, Extra Eyes , colorful fur and so on and so forth. They're hardly ever so disturbing in appearance that they become an Animalistic Abomination , however. Regardless of the outward details, though, the image is a stable one — these monsters are immediately recognizable for what they are when they appear, and the concept of a child fearing "a monster" in a closet or an attic immediately brings to mind images of these beasts. These creatures are usually depicted as spending their time lurking in creepy places and scaring people. Exactly why they do this varies greatly between portrayals, but generally tends to focus on them doing it either For the Evulz or because they physically need fear as some sort of resource, such as by being Emotion Eaters who feed on the fear that they cause. In some cases, they may even be creations of children's imaginations , existing as physical manifestations of their conscious or subconscious fears in a manner similar to Tulpas . Or maybe they just like eating kids. However, there are just as many benevolent depictions . Whether benevolent or malevolent, there is almost always some sort of connection to children. See also Things That Go "Bump" in the Night , which these monsters often are. More lighthearted examples are likely to overlap with Peek-a-Bogeyman . Compare Bigfoot, Sasquatch, and Yeti and the Swamp Monster , which these often resemble, although their origins and cultural connotations are for the most part unrelated.
